Destroying them would affect the prison — possibly freeing the Keeper, possibly releasing Tartuzi, possibly triggering catastrophe. Each fane destroyed has escalating consequences. The party will discover at least one during Act One.

details.label: Vallombrosa in Act Two
details.content: After Marcello's arrival (Phase Two threshold), Vallombrosa transforms from an external mystery into a living setting the party can explore. It has a population of fog children (and others), internal locations, and a geography shaped by the ancient di Errante estate. The Tragedy of Vallombrosa performed at the Amaranth Theater is a distorted artistic echo of the actual events.

connections.relationship: Guard the boundary between Venturia and the fog
Has been receiving fog children from Vallombrosa for over a thousand years
Imprisoned within Vallombrosa; its weakening imprisonment drives the campaign's supernatural crisis

history.dm_notes: Players know Vallombrosa as a spooky local legend. The reveal that it is an actual supernatural prison containing a still-living Archfey and a sphinx-turned-prison-lock should be genuinely revelatory. Phase One keeps it mysterious. Phase Two opens it as explorable territory.

history.name: Fog Children
history.summary: People who appear from Vallombrosa's fog with no memories of their lives before. They have been appearing in Venturia for over a thousand years, since the ancient tragedy. Liminal House was founded specifically to care for them.

details.label: What Venturia Knows
details.content: Fog children appear periodically from Vallombrosa with no memories. They are cared for by Liminal House. Noname's father was one. Orabella is one. The phenomenon is long-established and accepted as part of Venturia's unusual nature.

details.label: True Nature
details.content: Fog children are souls cycling through Vallombrosa's liminal space — the supernatural in-between created by the Keeper's imprisonment. Some are people who wandered into the fog years or centuries ago and emerged unchanged while time passed outside. Others may genuinely be reincarnated souls, reborn through the fog's connection to the space between lives.

details.label: Institutional Knowledge Suppression
details.content: Both Liminal House and Viro's Respite know more about fog children than they disclose to residents. When fog children ask about their experiences — particularly recurring first-person dreams of other lives — they are shut down. This suppression is deliberate and has been maintained across generations of institutional leadership.

details.label: Reincarnation Possibility
details.content: Orabella's recurring first-person dreams of being other people, and Valentro's muscle memory of sword fighting, may both indicate reincarnation — past lives surfacing through the fog's liminal properties. If some PCs are reincarnations of the original Emerald Tide adventuring party, this should emerge through play naturally rather than as a predetermined revelation.

connections.relationship: The institution founded to care for fog children
A fog child experiencing recurring first-person dreams of other lives
Noname's father was a fog child; Noname herself was raised at Liminal House after his death

history.dm_notes: The reincarnation mechanic is a long-game revelation. Handle carefully — it should create meaningful character moments and moral complexity, not feel like a plot twist imposed on players. Strange dreams, recognizing places they've never been, knowledge they shouldn't have — these should emerge naturally through play.

history.name: The Autumn Masquerade
history.summary: Venturia's most important annual cultural event, centered on the Amaranth Theater's staging of 'The Tragedy of Vallombrosa.' A city-wide masquerade tradition that carries genuine supernatural weight during the week before it occurs.

details.label: Public Significance
details.content: The most anticipated cultural event on the island. The Amaranth Theater stages a different director's interpretation of the Vallombrosa legend each year. The city debates productions for months. Everyone wears masks. The Masquers' Sodality is deeply involved in organizing the traditions.

details.label: Car's Trauma
details.content: Car witnessed a friend making a secret deal with an unknown third party before the Masquerade. The next night, she found her friend's body dead at the event. Her parents claim it was 'betrayal' but won't explain further.

details.label: Ferrier Prohibition
details.content: The Ferriers' Confraternity refuses to ferry passengers after midnight during the week before the Masquerade. Those who navigate the canals alone during those nights report becoming hopelessly lost as waterways seem to shift and fold until dawn.

details.label: Supernatural Significance
details.content: The Autumn Masquerade appears to coincide with a supernatural cycle connected to Vallombrosa's prison mechanics. The week before the Masquerade, the canals become genuinely dangerous. The theater itself feels 'more alive' during staging of the Tragedy. These are likely connected to the Keeper's domain (masks, identity) and the annual cycle of the prison's weakening or strengthening.

details.label: Silas's Plans
details.content: Silas has some agenda that climaxes at or around the Autumn Masquerade. What he is building toward and why the Masquerade is significant to his plans is an unresolved campaign question.

connections.relationship: Site of her friend's death; ongoing trauma and investigative thread
The Tragedy of Vallombrosa is staged here annually during the Masquerade
Silas has plans that climax at or around the Masquerade

history.dm_notes: The Masquerade is a strong Act One climax target — natural deadline for Silas's plans and a spectacular setting for major revelations. The canal behaviors during Masquerade week suggest it's a thin-boundary period when the supernatural is closer to the surface. Orabella may have been at the Masquerade when Car's friend died — explore this connection.

history.name: The History of Eldoria
aliases: The Age Before the Great Reunion
history.summary: The broader world history of Eldoria: a civilization sealed from the wider world for nearly five thousand years by the consequences of leaders who invented fake gods to reduce divine influence, and the Great Reunion that ended that isolation a generation ago.

details.label: The Sealing
details.content: Approximately 5,000 years ago, the rulers of Eldoria invented nine fake gods (the Church of the Nine) to reduce real divine influence over mortal affairs. Millions directing belief toward nonexistent gods weakened the true deities, who withdrew. The seas around Eldoria became impassable. Eldoria was sealed from the wider world of Eldyrin.

details.label: The Church of the Nine
details.content: For nearly five millennia, the fabricated religion was the dominant cultural and political force. The true gods were reduced to rumor. The Church did genuine good — hospitals, law, libraries — built on fraudulent theological foundations. The true deities worked quietly through the gaps, channeling power to those who believed themselves to worship false gods.

details.label: The Last Wrongs
details.content: An adventuring party called the Last Wrongs stood against Vecna's plot to bring a demon lord back to the material plane. Heroes from Faerûn (the wider world) crossed into the sealed continents and destroyed Vecna. In the aftermath, the Church of the Nine attempted to consolidate power, but the Last Wrongs and their allies exposed the Church's fundamental fraud.

details.label: The Great Reunion
details.content: When the Church's power collapsed, the true gods returned. The seas cleared. Ships from Faerûn and other regions sailed in. Eldoria discovered it had been isolated from a vastly larger world. The Great Reunion is a decade-long process of first contact, cultural shock, and theological reorientation that ended the sealed age.

details.label: Relevance to Venturia
details.content: The campaign is set in the post-Reunion era. The island of Seravalle and Venturia exist in this changed world where true deities have returned and the wider world is newly accessible. Garl Glittergold's direct interaction with Roxy reflects the restored divine presence. The Keeper of Faces is a pre-Reunion supernatural force whose imprisonment predates the world's current configuration.

history.dm_notes: The world history provides context for why divine patrons (Garl, the Keeper, Tartuzi's sphinx nature) function as they do. The restoration of true divine power is recent — the world is still adapting. The Keeper's imprisonment predates the Church, predates the Reunion, and predates everything the current civilization takes for granted.

history.name: The Bound
history.summary: Individuals in Venturia who have been supernaturally compelled — controlled without their consent. The Bound are appearing with increasing frequency, causing deaths, and driving the Council's current crisis response that has produced only gridlock.

details.label: What Is Known
details.content: Bound individuals appear throughout Venturia, compelled to act against their will. One Bound individual caused six deaths in a single incident. They are not criminals — they are victims. But they are also walking weapons regardless of intent.

details.label: Council Gridlock
details.content: The Bound Response Debate is a prime example of council dysfunction: Harbor-Lords want detention and examination. Guild-Masters want mandatory paid leave and care. Temple Authority wants sanctuary. Watch-Captain wants permanent detention. Nothing passes. The Watch detains them under vagrancy laws, causing protests. The problem escalates.

details.label: True Source
details.content: The Bound are created through supernatural compulsion connected to Silas and/or the weakening prison mechanics. Silas has the ability to create Bound individuals. The prison weakening may also cause spontaneous Bound phenomena without Silas's direct involvement.

details.label: Covenant's Perspective
details.content: The Covenant of Silver Scales is particularly disturbed by The Bound — supernatural compulsion represents the ultimate violation of free agreement, the antithesis of everything they believe about contract and consent.

connections.relationship: Silas creates Bound individuals; this is connected to his memory manipulation abilities

history.dm_notes: The Bound are both a symptom and a threat — they represent the prison's deterioration and Silas's activities simultaneously. The council gridlock around them is realistic and frustrating, providing space for the party to act outside official channels. The Covenant's concern about free agreement vs. compulsion is a philosophical thread worth developing.
